title: Improving Generated Release Notes Without Losing Customer-Facing Accuracy
summary: Focus improvements on preventing “release note drift” caused by stale PR text and late QA changes, and on making high-risk claims (migration/compatibility, security/retention/access control) easy to detect and explicitly approve. Use lightweight QA tagging plus a targeted review queue rather than expecting engineers or editors to rewrite everything.

claims.text: Add a pre-publication accuracy gate that compares generated notes to final QA deltas, because an audit found 14% of generated entries described behavior that changed after final QA and QA notes that PR text becomes unreliable after scope cuts.

Prioritize review of “commitment-like” statements (e.g., migration warnings, compatibility caveats, admin toggles) since customer success reported escalations when a generated note promised an admin toggle that was removed before release, and QA logs show an admin toggle removal and a late-added migration warning.

Implement a lightweight review queue that highlights changed/removed/sensitive claims instead of requiring manual maintenance of a second changelog, aligning with engineering’s willingness to tag QA deltas but not maintain parallel documentation.

Auto-flag and require explicit human approval for any customer-facing notes touching security, data retention, or access control, since compliance requires an approval record and wants sensitive statements easy to spot before publishing.

Update reviewer guidance to focus on drift-prone and high-impact items (changed claims, removed features, sensitive categories, and compatibility warnings) rather than rewriting every note, matching the existing editor checklist expectations.

openQuestions: What taxonomy/keywords will define “sensitive claims” and “commitment-like” statements for reliable auto-flagging (e.g., security/retention/access control; migration/compatibility; admin controls)?
Where should QA delta tags live (e.g., in the QA delta log only, in PR metadata, or both), and what is the required completeness before publication?
Who is the approver of record for compliance-sensitive notes, and what tooling is needed to capture and audit that approval?
What is the acceptable threshold for coverage vs. accuracy (e.g., allow omission of minor items to reduce drift risk)?
recommendation: Adopt a two-stage workflow: (1) generate notes from merged PRs but automatically diff/validate against the QA delta log and surface discrepancies; (2) route only highlighted items (changed/removed claims, migration/compatibility warnings, and compliance-sensitive statements) through a focused review queue with explicit approval for security/retention/access-control mentions. This targets the known drift sources while keeping reviewer effort lightweight.
